On the balance sheet, this company has about $10.66 billion in debt and around $152.4 million in cash. I view this debt load as being significant for a company with revenues of just over $9 billion, especially since revenues have been declining.

The Dividend And Share Buybacks

Constellation Brands pays a quarterly dividend of $1.02 per share, which totals $4.08 per share annually. The payout ratio is about 32.5% and it offers a yield of roughly 2.78%. In the current yield environment, I see a yield of less than 3% as not very attractive since this is close to what the current inflation rate is today. On April 9, 2025, it announced a $4 billion share buyback authorization. While this seems like a positive, plenty of companies have bought back shares only to see the share price go lower, and with a large debt load, I would prefer to see the company pay down its debt rather than buy back shares.

Short Sellers Seem To Expect More Downside

According to Shortsqueeze.com, just over 9.7 million shares are currently short, and this represents nearly 4 days' worth of average trading volumes. It seems the short sellers believe that negative sales trends could continue for longer than expected and that this could lead to P/E multiple compression. I see plenty of low to no-growth stock trading for 7 to 10 times earnings estimates, so this could be what the short sellers are targeting. With this stock still trading for nearly 13 times earnings, P/E multiple compression down to somewhere between 7 to 10 times earnings could suggest a lot of potential downside.

Alcohol Consumption Is Plunging

With RFK Jr. focused on making Americans healthy again or "MAHA," this could lead to policies and consumer awareness that lead to less alcohol consumption. This might compound the already set-in-place trends that have resulted in less alcohol consumption from teens to adults alike. As shown in the chart below, there have been ups and downs in the number of Americans who consume alcohol, but it has recently plunged to just around 54%, and this might not even be the bottom. There appear to be no signs of stabilization in this trend yet, and I believe it makes sense to see stabilization before considering an investment in this industry.

Why Constellation Brands Could Be A Value Trap

With a history of recent declines in revenues and a significant debt load that dwarfs the cash on the balance sheet, I believe this stock could be a value trap and even head lower. I believe there is also an opportunity cost that investors should consider since I see many other investment options that offer a higher dividend yield and even a lower P/E ratio. For example, take a look at Best Buy Co., Inc. (BBY) or HP Inc. (HPQ), both of which offer a yield of more than 5% and trade for a lower P/E ratio when compared to Constellation Brands.

As covered in my previous article on this company, Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.A), (BRK.B) was buying this stock back when Warren Buffett was in charge. I believe the idea that this famed investor was buying this stock has really helped to keep this stock from potentially even going lower. However, this raises the possibility that Berkshire Hathaway could announce the sale of this stock in the future (or perhaps an even bigger investment). If Greg Abel, who is the new CEO of Berkshire Hathaway, decides to sell this position, it could create a major selloff in this stock.

I believe health concerns are behind the trends that have resulted in less alcohol consumption; however, there has also been an explosion of new "vices" that might be competing for consumers' "feel good" budget, and this includes the burgeoning cannabis market. President Trump recently signed an executive order that is expected to expand the cannabis market even more in the years ahead, and this could just add more competition for the alcoholic beverage industry.

Where I Could Be Wrong

In the past, alcohol consumption trends have rebounded and even risen at times. If recent trends reverse, this could lead to bigger profits, and it could also create the case for P/E multiple expansion. Also, if Berkshire Hathaway continues to buy more shares, this stock could rise. In addition, if this company is successful in reducing costs while raising profit margins and managing the challenges of declining sales in the way that the tobacco industry has done for years, this stock could climb higher.

In Summary

I believe the recent rally in this stock probably just created a short-term pop that is likely to fade since industry trends remain challenging. I see better investment opportunities if you want a low-growth stock with a low P/E. I also see better places to invest if you are seeking yield. I see a significant opportunity cost in remaining a shareholder in this stock overall. At this point, I view this as a potential value trap, and I would wait to see some stabilization in alcohol consumption trends or a much lower valuation before considering this stock to be a buy.

data.title: Trump-Powell Battle Escalates: Will It Affect Markets?
data.description:
Late on Sunday, January 11, 2026, Chairman Jerome Powell released an explicitly worded and formal statement informing the world that he is under criminal investigation by the U.S. Department of Justice.

Powell was bold, assertive, and direct when he said the following:

This new threat is not about my testimony last June or about the renovation of the Federal Reserve buildings. It is not about Congress's oversight role; the Fed through testimony and other public disclosures made every effort to keep Congress informed about the renovation project. Those are pretexts. The threat of criminal charges is a consequence of the Federal Reserve setting interest rates based on our best assessment of what will serve the public, rather than following the preferences of the President.

In my view, if Powell is indicted, or if the investigation pressures him enough to cut rates, even when economic conditions are stable, markets may respond negatively. He is most likely aware of this, which may have prompted him to make the following statements:

This is about whether the Fed will be able to continue to set interest rates based on evidence and economic conditions—or whether instead monetary policy will be directed by political pressure or intimidation.

I have served at the Federal Reserve under four administrations, Republicans and Democrats alike. In every case, I have carried out my duties without political fear or favor, focused solely on our mandate of price stability and maximum employment. Public service sometimes requires standing firm in the face of threats. I will continue to do the job the Senate confirmed me to do, with integrity and a commitment to serving the American people.

Translation: Powell has clearly indicated that this threat (his words, not mine) is not substantial enough to deter him, and consequently the Fed, away from a data-driven approach.

Such an immediate and clear statement should ensure that market participants aren’t blindsided when global economies recommence activities on Monday.

The Fed Isn’t a Dictatorship

This escalation sets a concerning precedent for not only the future Fed chair but also present and future governors and regional Fed presidents - or anyone else involved in FOMC decisions, for that matter.

I believe that no individual in their right mind would prefer to be under investigation, especially a criminal investigation. So, can the possibility of such pressure in the future prove effective?

I don't think so because the Fed is not a dictatorship wherein the chairman can unilaterally decide future monetary policy.

But, in the 1970s, coordination did happen between U.S. President Nixon and Federal Reserve Chairman Burns, a case presented in this research paper titled How Richard Nixon Pressured Arthur Burns, published by the American Economic Association.

Arthur F. Burns was a close ally of Nixon who instructed him to engage in an expansionary monetary policy before the 1972 elections. Burns complied, even though there wasn’t any need to do so, by developing a framework advocating that monetary policy is not the driving force behind inflation.

Rate cuts were thus aimed at improving the labor market and quietly improving the odds of Nixon’s re-election. (Please read this paper for more details.)

But the Fed back in the 70s wasn’t as publicly transparent as it is now. Specifically, the Federal Reserve Reform Act of 1977 made the Fed more accountable and its actions more transparent after that Nixon-Burns episode led to stagflation in the 1970s.

That Reform Act directed the Fed to maintain stable growth, inflation, and employment and mandated that it present Congress with semi-annual reports on its monetary policy plans to achieve those objectives—a requirement still in place.

Lastly, the Fed now publicly announces its decisions, which was not the case until February 1995. These public policy announcements, and the Q&A sessions with established journalists that follow, serve as a strong deterrent for decisions that aren’t consistent with prevailing economic conditions.

If, however, a Fed Chair were to employ a policy inconsistent with economic evidence, market participants would pick up on that immediately and eventually price it in. In such a case, it is highly likely that markets react negatively and may not recover until the Fed’s credibility returns.

No More Rate Cuts

Lastly, I think that this would be an opportune time to discuss whether Powell would cut rates in the next FOMC meeting - irrespective of this latest investigation.

In my latest analysis on Bitcoin, I argued that rate cuts since September 2025 have been precautionary rather than stimulatory. And given that precautionary rate cuts, historically, have totaled 75 bps and that the latest Summary of Economic Projections doesn't indicate an imminent recession and uncontrolled inflation, further rate cuts are improbable

Additionally, "precautionary" indicates that Powell is cutting not because he wants to stimulate the economy or even the labor market, but to ensure that conditions don't deteriorate further.

As a result, even if unemployment and payrolls were to remain stable, which they have, as indicated by BLS’ latest data, with payrolls increasing by 50,000 and unemployment steady at 4.4 percent in December, Powell will halt the current rate cuts cycle.

In Closing

Nothing has changed: rate cuts are not required, and the Trump administration’s highly unusual actions against Powell, in my opinion, are inconsequential unless he has actually committed a criminal act.

Things are likely to go on as usual, and readers shouldn’t alter their portfolios based on this news alone.

data.title: Liberty Energy: The Pivot To A Power Company Is Cash Negative In The Near Term
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Liberty Energy Inc. (LBRT) is a mostly U.S.-focused oilfield services (or OFS) company that has been the second largest supplier of completions services (i.e., fracking), behind Halliburton Company (HAL). At least until recently.

Over the past quarters, management has aggressively pivoted from being an "oil company" to becoming a "power company." The idea is to bring Liberty's expertise in off-grid power solutions, acquired in powering electric frac and other oilfield operations, to a broader industrial customer base that includes data centers, among other use cases.

The company has branded the new offering as LPI, or Liberty Power Innovations:

Providing natural gas-fueled power generation and fueling solutions, LPI delivers dispatchable, distributed energy. Our generators are designed for modular deployment, supporting baseload, backup, and peak power needs, with fuel efficiency and low emissions that outperform conventional diesel alternatives. LPI’s ability to seamlessly integrate microgrid-ready solutions allows us to deliver scalable, adaptable power wherever it’s needed.

Source: Liberty Energy.

Connecting Liberty's story to the dominant AI/data center theme has been very positive for the stock price, which has outperformed the OFS sector (OIH):

The re-rating is not with rationale as the power business is less cyclical than frac demand and should merit a higher multiple.

The downside, however, is that LPI requires significant capex, which will push Liberty into negative free cash flow (or FCF) territory, resulting in higher debt. The investment may pay off, but it comes at the opportunity cost of giving up buybacks or growing the dividend. Further, any slowdown in the AI capex cycle could boomerang on Liberty's now higher multiples.

Considering both sides, I see Liberty as a "hold" at this time.

Don't write off the core frac business

The incentive to be considered an AI-enabling company is strong, especially given that oil has been in a bear market for three years now. U.S. frac fleets in particular keep sliding:

There are a couple of important trends under the surface, though. First, the headline frac spread count reported by Primary Vision is becoming less meaningful as an oilfield activity indicator. As completions designs evolve and operators now frac two, three, or even more wells at the same time while well lateral lengths and proppant loading increase too, the pumping horsepower packaged into the average fleet has grown:

The frac demand measured in horsepower has been more resilient than the frac fleet count.

Second, the frac supply has been bifurcated into older, diesel-run equipment and newer, dual-fuel and electric fleets that can run on natural gas and are preferred by operators from an efficiency perspective. Upgrading frac units from diesel to electric is not cheap, but for Liberty and a couple of other premium operators, this capex upgrade cycle is already in the past. This allows Liberty to maintain higher utilization for its fleets while smaller operators, unable to pay for these upgrades, are forced to idle or scrap their equipment.

When the U.S. shale oil cycle turns around, Liberty Energy will be incredibly well-positioned.

Analysts appear overly excited about LPI

Liberty's announcement last week on a potentially 1GW power deal with data-center operator Vantage was received very well by the market. Sell-side analysts have quoted $300k per megawatt annual EBITDA contribution, so a 1GW deal could theoretically add $300 million in EBITDA—quite material, given even at the top in 2023, Liberty's EBITDA was about $1.2 billion.

The Vantage buildout will be realized over at least five years, so this EBITDA won't come any time soon, but the data center news is so "hot" that analysts don't seem as interested in the core frac business that will continue paying the bills. In the Q3 earnings call, of about 15 questions asked in total, all related to LPI in some aspect, and zero had to do with frac fleets.

UBS, as reported by Seeking Alpha, believes LPI will contribute 28% of Liberty's EBITDA by 2028 and values the power business at $12 per share that is not yet "priced in."

Citi, also quoted by Seeking Alpha, puts the LPI contribution to 35-40% of EBITDA after the power business fully ramps up:

[Onsite] generation appears primed to ramp given accelerating AI investment, but at a pace that utility-scale additions would struggle to match. Onsite generation is the next technology that could scale, and we believe LBRT will participate.

Source: Seeking Alpha.

The sell-side price targets do not appear particularly aggressive, but the stock has also rapidly appreciated in recent months:

All this is not to say I do not believe LPI will succeed, but it is probably good to be a bit cautious when all analysts turn from bearish to bullish on news that may not materialize for a few years out.

Negative cash flow is the price of rebranding

What is quite certain, though, is that LPI will require significant capex investments for Liberty. In fact, consensus estimates now project negative cash flow until 2028, with the capex essentially being funded by debt increases:

While many energy companies have been gradually deleveraging, Liberty plans to rely on debt to fund its expansion into the power sector. In all fairness, much of that will be non-recourse project-level debt backed by power purchase agreements (or PPAs) with Liberty's data center customers:

Those project companies will be funded via debt that is backed by the PPA, ESA. Think about the fact that most likely, that will be project-specific debt, maybe around -- you could get to approximately 70% of the capital needs by debt. It will be nonrecourse to the corporation.

Source: Management comments on Q3 earnings call.

Still, these PPAs are only as good as the solvency of their counterparty. Even tech giants such as Oracle Corporation (ORCL) have seen debt pressures recently, so this will be an area to pay attention to.

By tying itself to the AI theme, Liberty will also benefit relatively less from an oil and gas rebound. The opportunity cost of the LPI capex lies in buybacks that didn't happen or dividends that didn't get paid out. In contrast, Patterson-UTI Energy, Inc. (PTEN), one of Liberty's main competitors in the frac space that is so far staying on the sidelines of the AI power business, is expected to maintain solid double-digit FCF yields over the next years.

Risks to consider

The core frac business remains exposed to oil and gas prices and oilfield capex.

The primary risk with the LPI business, in my view, is that we are too early in the AI journey to know for sure if the "hockey stick" power demand projections will materialize.

A recent Yahoo Finance article featured the AI race from China's perspective. While China has an advantage in terms of lower power costs and better infrastructure, the constraint for Chinese researchers has been the limited access to advanced chips and chip-making machines. This has, in turn, pushed them to develop more efficient algorithms that don't require as many chips.

My concern from LPI's perspective would be if power supply indeed becomes the limiting factor for AI developers in the U.S., it may also force American research teams to develop more efficient algorithms that require less power consumption. It is simply too early to tell how all this will play out.

Takeaway

I remain bullish on Liberty's core frac business given the company's dominant position as a supplier of premium equipment in the U.S. market and fading competition.

However, the trifecta of overwhelming Wall Street sell-side enthusiasm over the new LPI business, negative FCF that will be funded by debt, and the AI theme likely being overdue for some correction gives me pause before recommending Liberty as a "buy".

data.title: Carter's Stock Looks On Sale, But It Really Isn't
data.description:
Nearly two years ago, in February of 2024, I decided to downgrade shares of Carter's, Inc. (CRI) from a buy to a hold. This came after a window of time during which the stock underperformed the market. Financial performance was worsening, with revenue and profits declining. And even though shares of the company were attractively priced, a more bullish assessment did not adequately reflect the overall risks that I perceived. Fast forward to today, and things have gotten a lot worse. Revenue continues to drop. But in addition to this, the company is having to contend with a massive impact caused by tariffs.

The good news is that the stock is still cheap. But the bad news is that part of this is because the share price dropped 52% while the S&P 500 is up 39.1%. This is disappointing to say the least. Now, normally, as a value investor, I would be attracted to something like this. However, that's only the case when I can see the light at the end of the tunnel. And right now, with the data that's currently available, I am not convinced enough to take that leap. So, at best, all I can do is maintain it as a hold candidate.

Things have worsened

Given that we are in January of 2026, I don't think it would be terribly beneficial to focus too much on what happened before 2025. But so that you have the appropriate context, in the chart above, you can see financial results for the 2022 through 2024 fiscal years. The bottom line here is that revenue, profits, and cash flow metrics all declined for the business. I would have hoped that 2025 would have been better for the company. But if anything, it was more of the same, but on steroids.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first nine months of the 2025 fiscal year. Revenue of $1.97 billion was just a hair below the $1.98 billion the company reported the previous year. That's not an awful decline. And in fact, some parts of the company did quite well on the top line. Specifically, the U.S. Retail segment of the company reported a growth in revenue from $950.9 million to $956.3 million. According to management, this increase was driven by new retail stores that the company opened, a nice increase in the units per transaction both in its stores and online, and growth in its core baby assortment category. All of this is great to see.

This was not the only area of expansion that the company exhibited. The International segment reported a rise in revenue from $277.5 million to $289.8 million. This increase, according to the company, can be attributed to improved demand with its international wholesale partners, as well as growth that the company exhibited in Mexico and Canada. Mexico's expansion was driven by an increase in retail stores and a growth in traffic. And in Canada, the company improved because of higher traffic and a rise in units sold.

The problem for the business ended up being its U.S. Wholesale segment. This part of the company was hit, with revenue falling from $756 million to $726.9 million. Management blamed this on changes in the company's business model centered around its Simply Joys brand that, unfortunately, reduced visibility, traffic, and demand for the brand. Lower replenishment and demand with department stores, and a decline in the average sales price of the goods for the company, also proved problematic.

If all we were dealing with was a small revenue decline, I would be inclined to say that perhaps the company was finally stabilizing. But the big problem is on the bottom line. The firm went from a net profit of $124 million to a net profit of only $27.6 million. Other profitability metrics also took a hit. Operating cash flow was negative to the tune of $136.3 million. The year before, it was positive by $11.3 million. If we strip out changes in working capital, it was cut by almost half from $201.1 million to $106.7 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company dropped from $214.2 million to $127.4 million.

Some of these changes on the bottom line can be chalked up to foreign currency fluctuations, decreased prices for some items, and the like. It seems to me as though tariffs are the big pain point for the company. During the third quarter on its own, the company incurred around $20 million of tariff costs. Management mentioned in their earnings call for the third quarter that annualized incremental gross impacts from tariffs should be somewhere between $200 million and $250 million. For a company that generated net profits of $185.5 million in 2024, that is astronomical. Management is working to counteract this. For instance, the company is working on a $45 million cost reduction plan and has decided, as part of that, to increase the number of stores it is closing from 100 locations to 150.

Management is making other changes as well. It is working, for instance, to achieve cost-sharing agreements with some of its vendors. It is also shifting its production out of India and into other countries like Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, and Ethiopia. These are nations with lower tariffs. Price increases have also been implemented. And the company is even making changes to its product assortment and packaging. We don't really have much in the way of guidance for 2026. Management did say that we expect net revenue and earnings to improve year over year. But we really don't have specific details on the extent to which this would be the case.

With that in mind, if we project our results based on what we have seen already for the 2025 fiscal year, we would get net profits of about $41.3 million, adjusted operating cash flow of $152.4 million, and EBITDA of $203.3 million. Armed with these estimates and also with the historical results that the company achieved for the 2024 fiscal year, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. Honestly, except for the price-to-earnings multiple, these are very attractive multiples. This would normally elicit a very solid ‘buy’ rating from me. This is especially true considering that while the stock is expensive on a price-to-earnings basis compared to other similar firms, it is actually quite cheap compared to the competition when it comes to the other valuation approaches. In the chart below, I compared it to five such enterprises. Four of them are cheaper on a price-to-earnings basis. But only two were cheaper when it came to the price-to-operating cash flow basis, while one was cheaper on an EV-to-EBITDA basis.

Takeaway

As tantalizing as Carter's looks from a valuation standpoint, it's not a company that I can get behind right now. The stock is, quite frankly, rather risky. The tariff situation is disconcerting. Even though management has made promises regarding 2026, I need to see more details before getting excited about that. The firm has a history of declining revenue and profits before this point as well. And I do worry about a permanent decline on that front. In fact, it's only because of how cheap the stock is that I believe that assigning it a speculative ‘hold’ rating as opposed to something more bearish makes sense.
